retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiological basis for the precipitation of thyroid storms in patients with thyrotoxicosis is unclear. But, as mentioned above, a precipitating factor is always required to cause a thyroid storm. Several hypotheses have been proposed. One theory suggests the incidence of thyroid storm is due to the rapid increase in thyroid hormone levels rather than the absolute hormone level that occurs during thyroid surgery, following radioactive iodine treatment, after sudden discontinuation of the antithyroid drug, or after administration of the large dose of iodine in contrast studies. The hyperactivity of the sympathetic nervous system with increased response to catecholamine and an increased cellular response to thyroid hormone during acute stress or infections, causing cytokines release and altered immunological disturbances, are other possible mechanisms of thyroid storm. Most studies have failed to relate higher thyroid hormone levels as a cause of thyroid storm, except for the study by Brooks and others, which reported higher free thyroid hormone among the patients with thyroid storm. [9] In other words, the degree of thyroid hormone level is not directly related to a higher incidence of thyroid storm. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Per the Japanese National Survey, the incidence of thyroid storms was 0.2 per 100,000 people per year, about 0.22% of all thyrotoxicosis patients and 5.4% of hospitalized thyrotoxicosis patients. The average age of people with thyroid storm was 42 to 43 years, similar to those with thyrotoxicosis without thyroid storm. The male-to-female ratio for the incidence of thyroid storms was about 1:3, similar to that of thyrotoxicosis without storm groups. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple factors responsible for delayed diagnosis and treatment of this emergency condition: Unavailability of skilled thyroid surgeons Lack of suitable nuclear medicine units Lack of patient education Lack of internal physician communication

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is a rare presentation of hyperthyroidism. Thyroid storm accounts for about 1% to 2% of admissions for hyperthyroidism. As per the United States survey, the incidence of storms ranged from 0.57 to 0.76 cases per 100,000 per year in the normal population and 4.8 to 5.6 cases per 100,000 per year in hospitalized patients. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid storm, also known as thyrotoxic crisis, is an acute, life-threatening complication of hyperthyroidism. It is an exaggerated presentation of thyrotoxicosis, and it comes with sudden multisystem involvement. The mortality associated with thyroid storm is estimated to be 8 to 25% despite modern advancements in its treatment and supportive measures. [1] Thus, it is very important to recognize it early and start aggressive treatment to reduce mortality. The diagnosis of thyroid storm is clinical.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A thyroid storm is a true medical emergency that is fatal if left untreated. The cause of death may be heart failure, arrhythmias, or multiple organ failure. However, with treatment, most patients see an improvement within 24 hours. Risk factors for poor prognosis include: Advanced age Neurological deficits on admission [12] Failure to use beta-blockers and antithyroid medications Need for dialysis and/or mechanical ventilation

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Superimposed precipitating factors cause thyroid storms in patients with diagnosed or undiagnosed hyperthyroidism. It is more common with Graves disease but can occur with other etiologies of hyperthyroidism, such as toxic multinodular goiter and toxic thyroid adenoma. [2] The precipitating factors are: Abrupt discontinuation of antithyroid medicine Thyroid surgery Non-thyroid surgery Trauma Acute illnesses like infections, including COVID-19, diabetic ketoacidosis, acute myocardial infarction, cardiovascular accident, cardiac failure, and drug reactions [3] Parturition Recent use of Iodinated contrast medium Radioiodine therapy (rare) Burns Stroke or traumatic brain injury [4] Medication side effects, eg, amiodarone, anesthetics, salicylates [5] Hyperemesis gravidarum in pregnancy [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Determine the etiology of a thyroid storm. Assess the potential complications of a thyroid storm. Evaluate available treatment options for a thyroid storm. Communicate the importance of a well-coordinated, interprofessional team approach when managing patients presenting with thyroid storm. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: A thyroid storm, also known as a thyrotoxic crisis, is an acute, life-threatening complication of hyperthyroidism that presents with multi-system involvement. Despite modern advancements in its treatment and supportive measures, the mortality associated with thyroid storm is estimated to be 8 to 25%. Thus, it is very important to recognize it early and start aggressive treatment to reduce mortality. This activity reviews the tools available to assist with the diagnosis and management of thyroid storms and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical features are due to the exaggerated effects of the thyroid hormone. There is an intense metabolic activity that increases oxygen requirements. The resulting tachycardia to meet the oxygen requirements can induce heart failure and predispose the patient to arrhythmias. Similarly, CNS symptoms include irritability, seizures, delirium, and eventually coma.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Evaluation -- Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: These scoring systems are just guidelines. The actual diagnosis is based on clinical judgment. Based on the BWPS scoring system, a score of 45 or more is more sensitive but less specific than JTA scoring systems TS1 or TS2 to detect thyroid storm cases. A BWPS score of 25 to 45 may suggest an impending storm. A chest X-ray may help assess heart failure, and a head CT may help exclude a neurological cause in some patients. An ECG is often done to monitor for arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of thyroid storm consists of supportive measures like intravenous (IV) fluids, oxygen, cooling blankets, acetaminophen, and specific measures to treat hyperthyroidism. If any precipitating factors, for example, infection, are present, that needs to be addressed. Patients with thyroid storm must be admitted to the intensive care unit with close cardiac monitoring and ventilatory support if needed. [1] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Randomized trials are still being processed to determine the efficacy and safety of more medicines. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treating thyroid storms involves supportive measures like intravenous (IV) fluids, oxygen, cooling blankets, acetaminophen, and specific measures to treat hyperthyroidism. If any precipitating factors, for example, an infection, are present, they must be taken care of first. Patients with thyroid storm must be admitted to the intensive care unit with close cardiac monitoring and ventilatory support as needed. [1] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Storm -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with hyperthyroidism should be educated about the warning symptoms of thyroid storm, a serious but rare emergency. It is a dangerous disease, but early diagnosis and prompt treatment can improve the condition. Hyperthyroid patients should not delay visiting the emergency department after experiencing any symptoms. After the first crisis, non-compliant behaviors should be addressed, and treatment benefits and complications should be described. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
retrieved_docs.content: Triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin) is a thyroid hormone that is synthesized and secreted by the thyroid gland (štítná žláza). The štítná žláza is also the primary anatomical site where thyroid insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition characterized by reduced production of thyroid hormones, is identified. Recognizing the relationship between trijodthyronin and the štítná žláza, as well as the gland’s role in قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة, is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of disorders affecting thyroid hormone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 651
retrieved_docs.content: Adult respiratory failure, insufficiency and arrest are classified as specific respiratory disorders, while blood transfusion reaction (تفاعل نقل الدم) is a recognized complication of surgical or medical care. Abdominal pain is clinically associated with malignant hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث). Surgery is indicated for tumors, blood transfusion reaction, adult respiratory failure, thyroid disorders, altered sexual desire (الشهوة الجنسية), intestinal viral infection (عدوى بفيروس معوي), abdominal pain, malignant hypertension, follicular cyst, miscarriage (الاِختِنَاق), threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض) and paralysis (فالج). Pathophysiology elucidates mechanisms of these entities as well as thyroid disorders and sexual desire disturbances. Radiotherapy is an accepted modality for tumors, blood transfusion reaction, abdominal pain, follicular cyst, adult respiratory failure, thyroid disorders, intestinal viral infection and malignant hypertension, and is specifically indicated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors comprise a category that includes the subclass thyreostatika, which are agents specifically related to the function of the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). Both thyreostatika and the thyroid hormone trijodthyronin (triiodothyronine) are directly associated with the štítná žláza, reflecting their roles in modulating thyroid activity. The štítná žláza is also identified as the anatomical finding site for قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism), underscoring the clinical relevance of these substances and the gland in the diagnosis and management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2173
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy), reflecting its categorization within endocrine pathologies and the common surgical intervention used in its treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “chemically induced” functions as a qualifier for a range of medical entities. When applied to the category “Allowed qualifier,” it is associated with an increase in amniotic fluid (زيادة السائل الأمنيوسي (موه السلى)) and with thyroid disorders. In addition, the conditions “delayed movement disorder” (خلل الحركة المتأخر), “hyperlipoproteinemia” (فرط البروتينات الشحمية), and “aggressive breakfast” (فطار غزوي) can each be qualified by a chemically induced mechanism. This illustrates that chemically induced factors may underlie diverse obstetric, endocrine, neurological, metabolic, and dietary‑related presentations, highlighting the importance of recognizing chemical triggers across multiple clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2551
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is categorized as an اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is anatomically associated with head and neck structures. This connection highlights that thyroid malignancies arise within the endocrine system and involve the anatomical region of the head and neck, which is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor in the clinical management of تمزق, thyroid disorders, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that it may be used when documenting or planning treatment for each of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1543
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is linked to mortality for a range of conditions, including تمزق, thyroid disorders, التموت, الشهوة الجنسية, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, ورم عضلي أملس, and بتر حوضي شقي. The same allowed qualifier is also associated with the provision of care (ošetřování) for each of these conditions, indicating that it serves as a recognized factor in both the risk of death and the management strategies for تمزق, thyroid disorders, التموت, الشهوة الجنسية, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, ورم عضلي أملس, and بتر حوضي شقي. In addition, lung disease due to external agents is connected to mortality, highlighting its potential to contribute to fatal outcomes, and it is likewise related to care (ošetřování), underscoring the importance of therapeutic interventions for this respiratory condition. Together, these relationships emphasize that the allowed qualifier functions as a common element influencing both the prognostic (mortality) and therapeutic (care) aspects of a diverse set of medical disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: may be identified in relation to the thyroid gland, reflecting pituitary‑thyroid axis cross‑talk. Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology and GDC Value Terminology each incorporate the concepts Thyroid Extract and septicemia (except in labor), indicating that both terminological frameworks recognize and classify these specific medical entities. The inclusion of Thyroid Extract reflects coverage of an endocrine‑related extract, while the inclusion of septicemia (except in labor) demonstrates that both systems address a severe systemic infection, explicitly excluding the obstetric context. By sharing these concepts, the two terminologies provide consistent reference points for documentation, coding, and analysis across healthcare settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1749
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة, الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل, and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل. These represent drug dependence before delivery, rubella infection (German measles) occurring during gestation, and thyroid dysfunction arising in the course of pregnancy, respectively. Recognizing these conditions as part of other complications of pregnancy underscores the importance of assessing substance use, preventing and managing rubella infection, and monitoring thyroid function to protect maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology serves as an important qualifying domain for a range of substances and clinical conditions. Cannabis can be qualified by microbiology, indicating that microbiological assessment is relevant to its safety, composition, or therapeutic use. In addition, microbiology is an allowed qualifier for blood‑transfusion reactions (تفاعل نقل الدم), for thyroid disorders, and for threat of miscarriage (تهدید بالإجهاض). This underscores the role of microbiological evaluation in diverse areas of medicine, from ensuring the quality of cannabis products to assessing the safety of transfusions, the pathophysiology of endocrine disorders, and the risk factors affecting pregnancy outcomes. Integrating microbiological insights into patient care therefore enhances diagnostic accuracy, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making across these varied contexts.
